speaker: interviewer
text: This is the interview of Louis Stepak. It is taking place in his home in Vineland, New Jersey. The date is November 6, 1996. My name is Ari Goldberger, and this is tape one. My name is Ari Goldberger, and I'm conducting an interview with Mr Louis Stepak in his home in Vineland, New Jersey. The date is November 6, 1996. Could you please state your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Louis, L-O-U-I-S S-T-E-P-A-K, second name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stepak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stepak, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your date of birth, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're 81.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can I call you Louie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Louie, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Louis, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kolki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kolki? And what country is that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Poland, near Lutsk. It's part of Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and Kolki is spelled how? Is that K-O-L-K-I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Kolki, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me the name of the-- so was Lutsk, Lutsk the largest-- is that a big town near, near Kolki?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very big town, yeah. Poland, Lutsk. It's a big one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that, is that closer to Russia or to Germany? Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's far away for Russia and Germany

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the east-- in the east of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: East of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you also grew up in Kolki?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your earliest memories of Kolki.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very, very nice. We grow there. We have a lot of friends, and we were going to school together. Going there was a nice bridge, so every Saturday, we walk in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bridge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was two bridges in Kolki, We always, Friday night and Saturday night, we liked to walk there. Saturday daytime, we meet each other, we know each other. We know each other, like every each one is like together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, the bridges went over what rivers, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- now, they say, there's no, no river there. Everything dry up now. They was calling-- one was calling the river [INAUDIBLE] River, and the other one was calling Styr, like deeper. But now, they says there's nothing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, you as a boy, would meet your friends there? And would the family go too?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were swimming there together in that Styr, and that lake. Day, night, summertime. it was a life. It was a real good life. Real good life. If, if you meet now some people from my friends-- I got them in Israel and I got a few in here, right here in America. We always come together, and, and sometime, we're talking about how was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So you went swimming, and do anything-- did you play cards there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, we played cards. We swim. Even some of us, there was swimming and win a medal, too, for now people. There was a race, something in the ocean. And the swimmer, him, Yankel was the name, I remember. He's not alive. He's no more alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the, the home you grew up in. Was it an apartment, a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we have a house. We have a house, we have a big mortgage to pay on the house. Yeah, but we survive. And we had a grocery there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, your grocery, this was your father's, father's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father and mother, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zissa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-I-S-S-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hiya.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had this grocery store. Was the grocery store near your house, or was it part of--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the house, in the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody got a grocer in the front, and in the back was the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of things did you carry in the grocery store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, was not too much like in America, but it was a grocery. They have kerosene we used to sell. Flour. We used to sell a lot of little things, even bulbs, for light bulbs I remember. Even matches we sell. It's not a big deal, but we survived. Candies. Other things like that. Not too big, but we survived. It was good. We make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm trying to get a picture of what-- of what Kolki was like. Were there cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: No cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: No cars. If you got around, it was either walk or bike?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything is walking. Walking distance. They have horses. Horses with wagons. And even bulls with wagons. But we didn't have that. We had-- hire a, hire a man, and you take us with the horses, and we pay for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have a phone? A telephone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody have telephones in Kolki?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- and the heating. Was the heating by gas? Oil?

speaker: interviewee
text: The heating is only wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wood.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody got a home with woods, and we live with that. This is an old, very hard life, but we live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you sell--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we was happy. We was happy. We live and was happy. Were thinking, it's not-- can't be better than that. Can't be better than that. We were very happy. Young boys would come out with the girls, and was dancing, and going on parties. And everything was beautiful. Everything was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever travel outside of Kolki as a-- as a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Lutsk. I used to learn the yeshiva in Lutsk in the New Year. Lutsk yeshiva done alone, and [INAUDIBLE] yeshiva when I was a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your-- the schools you went to. Did you start off in yeshiva in Lutsk, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeshiva, yeah. But over there, you don't go in high school. I don't go on high school. I don't go in [INAUDIBLE] because you have to pay a lot of money for that. I have a private teacher and yeshivas.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Lutsk from Kolki.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, was overnight drive with a pair of horses, over night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you went to school, did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, I stayed there. There was-- there was taking-- like everybody give a day food, and we sleep in the yeshiva there, in the same building. In like seven days, I have seven bosses where they feed me. And every boy do same thing. They help out the yeshiva. Like in Lutsk, you stay in there, but not everybody takes a-- takes a key to feed him. One day a week. In Lutsk, you Sunday by that place, Monday in another place. And this way was gone, and this way was staying at the yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- so in other words, you ate someplace different every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: In someone's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In someone's house. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it all boys in the yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mostly boys. Mostly boys, no girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now as a child, did you ever go to, to a different school?

speaker: interviewee
text: A child, it was a cheder, a Jewish cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Kolki?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kolki, yeah. I got even and my friend who's in, in Brooklyn. I went with him same cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- tell me about the Jewish community in Kolki. Was it just Jews living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was Jews and Gentles. It was avery nice, nice city. It was a good thousand families.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1,000 Jewish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Families, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What percentage of all of Kolki was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, less than the Ukraine. It's just less, less. There was more. But it was separate. There was the city like Jewish, and you have the city like the Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say the Ukraine? Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainian, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that-- it was Jews and Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukraine, and we lived there together, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did most of the Jews support themselves in Kolki?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot have groceries. They have-- they're working. They was working. There was tailors, shoe makers with horses, makes for the, for the horses. Everybody got a trade Everybody got a trade. The most, they have trades. That's it, was survival. Everybody lives. Everybody makes a living. And there was a few stores, too. Like all kinds of stores, grocery, like clothes, clothes, let's see, like a harvest all year. Mostly everybody got-- it was good. It was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home? Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you speak Polish outside of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, yeah. Polish, Yiddish, mostly Yiddish. When you come to Polish, we talk Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the, the Ukraines. Were they speaking Polish or Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: They speak Ukrainian and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we belong there to Maccabi. Maccabi. It was name Maccabi. Come together everybody, and we was playing football together. All young, young, the young ones. All young one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about-- were you religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, yes. My father and mother was religious, very religious. And I have to keep on with them. There's no way-- other way. You can't go without a cap. You can't go-- you can't do with-- Saturday was closed. The business close and everything. You've got to do what do parents tell you. There's not other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the relationship between Jews and non-Jews in Kolki.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, was everything was all right. Was all right. You know, like everything was normal. Sometime maybe they have little fights, the youngsters. But it's nothing in between. Still, they know that's the Jews, and that's the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was anti-Semitism. They show up a little bit, but not too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me, tell me, tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes they was talking, there was talking about the Jews. They don't do nothing. They have to work. Everything was all right until before the Germans come.

speaker: interviewer
text: But tell me, did anybody-- did you ever have a situation where someone called you a name, or-- before the Germans came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much. They like me, because later, I got a grocery. I went-- we'll come to that. I went-- we have a grocery in Kolki. In Poland, was very hard to get married a girl, a girl like you have. You have to them-- they call in Jewish, [YIDDISH], money. If you want to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a dowry?

speaker: interviewee
text: A dowry, right. Other way is not America, where you get married either way. We got a daughter, right? I got a sister. I had a sister, and an older brother, and a younger brother. And she have, in the meantime, she got a boyfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, one second. Your sister's-- the sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lehke, Lehke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lehke?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lea, Lea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lea.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a brother, Mayer, the younger than me. And an older brother, [INAUDIBLE], Isaac. And she have to get married. And we don't have much money to give. Then she wanted me just sign-- the, the son-in-law. My son-in-law, well, you have to be the son-in-law, he wanted me to sign him, I'm going to give him $50, like you say, and-- what do you say.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was going to be your brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, brother-in-law, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: He wanted a dowry?

speaker: interviewee
text: A dowry. Then I have to sign myself. Sign him I'm going to pay him off $50. $50. That time, $50 in Europe was a lot of money. And the time to pay him off, because I was going in the business, too. In the grocery. And I signed. Then we give him away the house. We give him away the house, some of the house with the business. And I went with my father and mother, with my younger son went to a smaller town. A smaller dorf, like same thing. He was there, Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, you said with your younger son. You were with your younger brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The younger brother, younger brother, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was Isaac.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Isaac is the older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and the younger, Mayer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mayer, Mayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mayer, Mayer. So you went with Mayer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mayer and my father and mother. And we open up a grocery in Matejka. They call it Matejka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that another town?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's another small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Matejka. Matejka-- M-A--

speaker: interviewer
text: M-A-T-E-J-K-A.

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- Matejka, OK. Is-- and the older brother of mine was married there in Kolki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Isaac.

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac was married there in Kolki. Not too far. And he opened a grocery, too. Is-- I forgot what I want to say. Then-- OK. Then we went there. When they [INAUDIBLE], and we give away my sister, the house, and everything. We went there. We opened a grocery. We start surviving there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Matejka.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Matejka, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was that from Kolki?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a, a good three hours' walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, I was young. Maybe 20, 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's already-- ready like 1930. Early 1930s.

speaker: interviewee
text: '30, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it-- tell me about what the Jewish holidays were like for you and the family. Did a lot of people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. We enjoyed the Jewish holidays. Very good. We have time there. It's not America. We had plenty of time to go to the synagogue, and pray, and come back, and, and have supper, and have dinner. There was very enjoyment. Very enjoyment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get together with outside-- with bigger family members, with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, sure. Everybody, it was like one family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come to your house to celebrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they come to-- there was [INAUDIBLE] would come, every each one to each house. But the other way, it was like, like one family. Old Kolki was like one family. There's not such thing of one don't know the other one. Or it's like one, one family, I'm telling you, was our life. And everybody was not too rich. It was poor. Make a living, trying to make a living. And we was happy. I wish we there now. I miss staying there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't make bar mitzvahs.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we don't make bar mitzvahs. They give just-- sometimes, the synagogue, they give a piece of cake and a bottle of whiskey. There was big-- it was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I don't mean a big party. But did you go, and you pray on when you were 13, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they give me maftir, and that's it. They don't make big deals like here now. Not big deals, no. In Kolki, especially. In Kolki, I don't know the other towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a boy growing up in Kolki, what were your plans for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was the future, nothing else. There was [INAUDIBLE], and religious. You make a living. The main thing is to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you figured you're just going to stay in Kolki for the rest of your life, and you're going to have a grocery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Have a grocery and be happy. And we was-- and we was happy. We was happy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the-- do you remember the Gentile holidays, the Christian holidays? Christmas and Easter. How did you feel? What was that like as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We don't feel-- that's a day holidays. That's a day they're religious, and this is our religious. We don't feel the other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no particular anti-Semitic incidents around Easter?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was. There was. There was from them. There was, especially Christmas, when they come some other days. There was. Well, everything was-- everything was a law. Was Poland was taking care of that. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Uh oh. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Everything. And that's the way it was. In America, we got too. We got it here, too, but we don't know it. We don't hear. But a lot of anti-Semitism here too.

speaker: interviewer
text: But tell me-- we'll talk about that later. But tell me-- tell me specifically, what would you call anti-Semitic incident happening in Kolki? Somebody called names? Did anybody beat anybody up? Did anybody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, beat up, that happened just with boys. With boys and girls, maybe, they beat up, but not-- not the older ones, no. Never happened, no. I don't think so. Very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: So nothing that really bothered you.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was bothering me that we see Poland alone was against the Jews. Poland like, let's see. I got a grocery, and a Polish man have a grocery. Each sending the kids and pick up my grocery in the front, not to go to me to buy. This show up from the Polish people. That time, was not showing up much from the Ukraine. From the Polish more, and was the anti-Semitism. And we saw it lately. That's another way. But we don't have other choice. That's the way it was going. That's where we saw it. It was pick-- and the Polish-- the Polish boys was bothering more time than the Ukrainian. Only Polish. And they would send us to Palestine. Go to Palestine. Palestine, you have nothing to do here. I got a grocery-- well, I got a grocery in [INAUDIBLE], and I got a [INAUDIBLE]. And the people like me. They don't like him. He was-- he was doing a lot of bad things to me, a lot of bad things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like one time, he put me in with another Polish together. And they put me in like I'm a communist. And they come in, and they put in a communist. We don't know what's going on. And the police coming in and start looking the house. And they find under the-- under the-- where we keep always for the cows. It's a big, big cans to feed them, the cows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cows.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he put in that thing under that-- under that bucket. And we don't know. The police come in and start looking. They're looking all over. They can't find it. Then they went out back and ask the man, you say he's a communist. We can't find it. He says, go under the barrel, and you'll find it. Then they went in the barrel, and they took me, and they arrest me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they-- what was under the can? Like a document?

speaker: interviewee
text: They say a document, and paper, I'm a communist. Like UGA or something. They put it in. And they took me away. You know, that time it was a big shock. They took me away to the police, and the police there took me to Kolki that time. And there was all in the whole town, the whole Kolki was mixed up. They know I'm not a Communist. I'm, I'm a religious man. I'm my religious-- all of a sudden I'm a Communist. They keep me there for a week, and after that, then they let me out, and I come back home. That's-- the Polish people was more against than the Ukraine at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this guy who set you up, the Polish man who owned the grocery store. Did you-- you knew it was him that did it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you confront him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I confront him, but I couldn't-- I couldn't do nothing to him. He was a Polack and the police Polacks. I just took-- one Ukraine was mixed up too with them. The Ukraine, it'd-- and one of them a Polack, I sued him later. And I find him, and I put him in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah? The Ukraine?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukraine. One Ukraine and on Polish people, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You put them both in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a court-- because the police was with me. The police man, he couldn't stand it. He say to me, Louis, wait. Just, just wait. He knows. When he come out back, he told him go under the-- well, how you know he's laying there? Then he was the witness. And he was in court. He was saying that, too. On my side. He was a very good officer. There was a good officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Polish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were good and bad Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good and bad, yeah. He was a very good one. And we put him in jail after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the guy who you put in jail who owned the grocery?

speaker: interviewee
text: One, oh, Polokovsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polokovsky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polokovsky, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it? Or no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, how you spell-- how you spell it is good. No difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polokovsky. He was mean. He was mean, and the woman, too. They have to be-- I don't know, maybe they have-- they don't like him. The Ukrainian people don't like him. I got Polish people not too far. They used to go under the lake and come to me to the grocery. They don't like them. That's not my fault that they like me and I was doing the business. When they come to business before Passover, I was-- I was selling 20 bags of flour, because everybody buying. He bought three, and he never sell the three, even. You see, that's-- that's-- he was jealous, and mean, and trying to do something to me. That's, but-- but the most is the Polish people started up. They started up against the Jewish, too, that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this incident happened around, what, 1930? Sometime in the early 1930s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was not too far before the war, before the war. It's not too far before the war, 1935, '36, '37. Did

speaker: interviewer
text: You--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Around that time, did you know what was going on in Germany with Hitler? Did you hear about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, every day. We have every day. We was every day worried.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get your news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have the news there. The radio, we had a radio, at least on the radio. Not everybody got a radio, but somebody got a radio, and one told the other one what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you heard about when Hitler came to power? And how early did you learn of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the same time, we heard that. We-- it was-- it was normal. We heard that. We know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any relatives in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any relatives in other parts of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have in Lutsk, in Rivne, my father's-- my [NON-ENGLISH], what do you call my father's mother. And there was a few cousins there. But we didn't see each other. We couldn't get in touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember hearing about Kristallnacht in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh yes, the Kristal night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that make you feel? Did you feel threatened? Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody feel threatened. Everybody, They couldn't eat. They couldn't drink. Everybody was-- it's unbelievable. Nobody believed that it can happen, that. Nobody. They were changing-- they were changing the clothes. It was not like police doing. No, in the beginning, it was not like police, in breaking the stores and everything that were there. Put in-- take live people, and put them in, and burn down everything right away. And that's it. That's the problem started. Everybody saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you felt that you were threatened in Kolki?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Did you think the Germans were going to come, or did you think that the Poles could do the same thing to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we're not afraid for-- the Poles was waiting for that, but they wouldn't do it because the Poles alone was afraid for German. They says there was something-- they were saying there were something in Polish government was a German. They-- they don't know it. They were saying, I don't know. I remember, I remember that time. Well, we know-- we know it's a problem. We know the problem. And a lot of-- a lot was saying already, I wish Hitler comes first here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poles were saying that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Poles, the Ukrainians were saying. Yeah, they were saying. They want him, because they don't like the Russian. They don't like the Russian. They want to have the German. And when the Germans come, they come with open hands against them to get rid of the-- of the Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any talk amongst your family, or other people in Kolki, about leaving, going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't even think about it. That's the-- that's the trouble, we should be thinking about it. To run with the Russians. To run with the Russians. Leave? You can't leave nowhere. Not with the Russians. Where can you leave? There's nowhere to go. How can we go from Kolki? Where we going to go?

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't think--

speaker: interviewee
text: They can't go from Lutsk or Rivne, the bigger-- bigger people with more money, they can hire horses and go someplace or with the airplane somewhere. But we in Kolki, we don't even-- we couldn't think about it. We just wait, what can happen?

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you now about when you first heard of the outbreak of World War II with the Germans invading Poland in, in September of 1939. I'm sure you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- what can I tell you? How we feel, especially the Jewish people? What can we tell? It's nothing-- it's nothing-- it's unbelievable what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect your life when Hitler invaded Germany in 1939? Now I know you were on the Russian side, so you didn't-- when the Germans invaded in '39, they didn't come to Kolki yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you still-- well, in one way, we was very happy. One way, we was happy. At least we are alive by the Russian. But the other way, we don't know-- we didn't know Germans were going to come on top on the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, when did the Russians come in to Kolki?

speaker: interviewer
text: They come in right away. When they start, right away the come in. When the Germans came in, the Russians came in Kolki?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came in. [INAUDIBLE], right away, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your life change under the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we manage. We manage with the Russians. We manage. At least they didn't kill nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take your grocery store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we closed. There was no more grocery. No more nothing. Everything is closed. No business. If somebody wanted to have a business, you have to pay 20 times more. You couldn't stay in the business. Everything is-- was liquidated when the Russians come. But they don't kill nobody. In, in the Ukraine, it's-- the Ukraine and the Polish people can do nothing to you. I remember I was in a meeting, and we say, when we can get something like clothes, or material from the Jewish-- from the Jewish people. And they called them [NON-ENGLISH], they call.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, [NON-ENGLISH]? Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jew. And the man where he was working with the Russian. He says, we don't have [NON-ENGLISH]. We have [NON-ENGLISH]. It's nicer. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Hebrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hebrew, Hebrew, right. And they shut up him. And he couldn't talk. And the Russian, at least they didn't kill nobody. It was everything fine. We even managed-- we, we don't-- we don't-- it was not so great a war, but at least not killing. It was a lot of refugees coming in to us. There was a lot of refugees from Germany come in in Kolki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where'd they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Germany. When Germany come in, they were very, very intelligent people. Very intelligent.

speaker: interviewer
text: German Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: German Jews, yeah, They have right away jobs with the Russians. They give them jobs and everything. And it was good. Even, even--
